straight_beat.name: Reuben Tachibana
straight_beat.column:
The A’s took the series. The move to Sacramento is still pending. Those are the facts.  

Last night’s 1-0 loss to the Yankees wasn’t the kind of game that sticks in your memory. It wasn’t a meltdown, not exactly. It was a quiet, methodical surrender. The rotation gave up three earned runs, but not a single one crossed the plate. The offense, again, did what it does: stood around, waited for the pitch, and then blinked. The ledger says otherwise.  

Let’s dissect this. The A’s pitching staff has been a mixed

optimist.name: Penny Halloran
optimist.column:
The Athletics didn’t just beat the Yankees yesterday—they reminded us why we’re still here, clinging to the hope that this team, this city, and this coliseum aren’t just relics of a bygone era. A 1-0 win, a bullpen that didn’t fold, and a lineup that found a way to scratch out a run when the lights were brightest. Sure, the standings say 5-7 and 2.0 games behind, but let’s not mistake the scoreboard for the soul of this organization.  

You look at Soderstrom’s cut and tell me we’re not two years from playing October baseball, wherever that field happens to be. That kid’s swing? It’s the kind of thing that makes you believe in the process. He’s not just a prospect; he’s a reminder that the game is still being played here, that the A’s still have the kind of depth that can turn a single run into a memory of resilience. And let’s not forget about the pitching staff. They’re not just throwing strikes—they’re throwing *meaning*. The way they held the Yankees’ lineup in check, the way they didn’t let the pressure of a late-inning deficit break their backs… that’s the kind of grit that builds teams.  

Now, I’ll be the first to admit the road ahead is long. The standings are a mirror, and they’re not flattering. But here’s the thing: this team isn’t built on shortcuts or quick fixes.

pessimist.name: Gil Mosbacher
pessimist.column:
The A’s lost again. Another 1-0 decision, another reminder that the AL West is a graveyard for teams that can’t find their way out of the dugout. The scoreboard reads 5-7, but the real numbers are uglier: 2.0 games behind, a .385 team batting average, and a bullpen that looks like it’s been assembled by a guy who thinks “dominant” means “dominoes.” This isn’t just a losing season—it’s a slow-motion collapse, orchestrated with the precision of a man who believes that “strategic patience” is the same thing as “strategic incompetence.”  

Fisher out. Always Fisher out.  

You can’t blame the players for the way this team moves. They’re trying. The kids in the minors are getting called up, the veterans are showing grit, and the starters are throwing strikes. But none of it matters because the front office has turned the A’s into a revolving door of prospects traded before they’re ready and veterans dumped before they’re useful. Last week, they sent another kid to Toronto for a guy who can’t hit lefties. Yesterday, they let a reliever go, even though he’d closed games for them last season. It’s not a plan—it’s a spreadsheet of every player Fisher didn’t pay.  

The real tragedy isn’t that the A’s are losing. It’s that they’re losing with such quiet, corporate indifference. You’d think a team that once had the
